name: Essential Hypertension Management Protocol
description: A comprehensive, evidence-based protocol for the management of essential hypertension (ICD-10: I10) in primary care settings, adhering to the 2017 ACC/AHA Hypertension Guidelines.

templateData.duration: Initial treatment phase: 3-6 months for blood pressure control. Ongoing: Lifelong management.
goals: Achieve and maintain a blood pressure target of <130/80 mmHg for most adults.
Reduce cardiovascular morbidity and mortality.
Prevent target organ damage (e.g., kidney disease, stroke, heart failure).
Improve patient adherence to lifestyle modifications and pharmacotherapy.
Minimize adverse effects of antihypertensive medications.

items: Initiate pharmacotherapy for Stage 1 Hypertension (BP ≥130/80 mmHg) if ASCVD risk ≥10% or existing CVD/diabetes/CKD.
Initiate pharmacotherapy for Stage 2 Hypertension (BP ≥140/90 mmHg) for all patients.
Consider combination therapy (two first-line agents from different classes) for Stage 2 Hypertension or if BP is >20/10 mmHg above target.
Titrate medications every 2-4 weeks until target BP is achieved or maximum tolerated dose is reached.
Educate on the importance of adherence to medication and lifestyle changes.
Provide guidance on home blood pressure monitoring techniques and log keeping.
Discuss potential side effects of medications and when to seek medical attention.
Explain the risks associated with uncontrolled hypertension.
Regularly assess blood pressure in office and encourage home monitoring.
Monitor for signs and symptoms of target organ damage (e.g., kidney function, retinopathy, left ventricular hypertrophy).
Assess for medication adherence and potential barriers.
Screen for secondary causes of hypertension if indicated.

medications.name: Thiazide-type Diuretic (e.g., Chlorthalidone, Hydrochlorothiazide)
medications.instructions: First-line for most patients. Take in the morning. Monitor electrolytes (potassium, sodium) and renal function.

medications.name: ACE Inhibitor (e.g., Lisinopril, Ramipril)
medications.instructions: First-line, especially in patients with diabetes, CKD, or heart failure with reduced ejection fraction. Monitor renal function and potassium. May cause cough or angioedema.

medications.name: Angiotensin Receptor Blocker (ARB) (e.g., Losartan, Valsartan)
medications.instructions: First-line, particularly if ACE inhibitor is not tolerated due to cough. Monitor renal function and potassium. Contraindicated in pregnancy.

medications.name: Calcium Channel Blocker (CCB) (e.g., Amlodipine, Diltiazem ER)
medications.instructions: First-line, especially for African Americans or if ACEI/ARB is contraindicated. Dihydropyridine CCBs (e.g., amlodipine) are preferred. May cause peripheral edema.

lifestyle: Sodium Reduction: Limit dietary sodium intake to <1500 mg/day (or at least a 1000 mg/day reduction).
DASH Diet: Adopt a Dietary Approaches to Stop Hypertension (DASH) eating plan.
Weight Management: Achieve and maintain a healthy body weight (BMI 18.5-24.9 kg/m2).
Physical Activity: Engage in at least 90-150 minutes/week of aerobic exercise and dynamic resistance exercise 3 times/week.
Alcohol Moderation: Limit alcohol intake to no more than 2 drinks/day for men and 1 drink/day for women.
Smoking Cessation: Advise and assist all smokers to quit.
Stress Management: Implement stress-reducing techniques (e.g., meditation, yoga).
Potassium Supplementation: Encourage increased dietary potassium intake unless contraindicated by kidney disease or medication.

followUp.frequency: Initial follow-up 2-4 weeks after initiating or changing therapy. Monthly until BP is controlled. Then every 3-6 months for stable patients.
metrics: Fundoscopic exam (for hypertensive retinopathy, as indicated)

warnings: Avoid ACE inhibitors and ARBs in pregnant women or those planning pregnancy due to teratogenicity.
Monitor for orthostatic hypotension, especially in elderly patients or those on multiple antihypertensives.
Avoid non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) with ACE inhibitors/ARBs/diuretics due to risk of acute kidney injury.
Beta-blockers are not first-line for uncomplicated hypertension unless there is a compelling indication (e.g., post-MI, heart failure).
Caution with potassium-sparing diuretics or potassium supplements in patients on ACE inhibitors/ARBs due to hyperkalemia risk.
Be aware of potential drug interactions with over-the-counter medications (e.g., decongestants, NSAIDs).
Patients with secondary hypertension require specific management beyond this protocol.
Rapid reduction of blood pressure can be dangerous; aim for gradual control unless in a hypertensive emergency.

name: Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus Management Protocol
description: An evidence-based clinical protocol for the comprehensive management of Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus (T2DM) in primary care, adhering to the ADA 2024 Standards of Care in Diabetes.

templateData.duration: Initial phase: 3-6 months for glycemic stabilization and lifestyle modification. Ongoing: Lifelong management.
goals: Achieve and maintain individualized HbA1c target (e.g., <7.0% for most adults, <6.5% for select individuals, <8.0% for those with comorbidities/hypoglycemia risk).
Achieve and maintain blood pressure <130/80 mmHg (for most adults with T2DM and hypertension).
Achieve and maintain LDL-C <1.8 mmol/L (<70 mg/dL) for patients 40-75 years with ASCVD or multiple risk factors.
Achieve and maintain body weight reduction of ≥5% for overweight/obese individuals.
Prevent or delay the progression of diabetes-related complications (e.g., nephropathy, retinopathy, neuropathy).
Minimize hypoglycemia risk and improve quality of life.

items: Referral to Medical Nutrition Therapy (MNT) with a Registered Dietitian Nutritionist (RDN).
Structured physical activity program (e.g., 150 minutes/week of moderate-intensity aerobic activity, 2-3 sessions/week of resistance training).
Smoking cessation counseling and support.
Stress management techniques and mental health screening.
Regular self-monitoring of blood glucose (SMBG) or continuous glucose monitoring (CGM) as indicated.
Metformin initiation (if eGFR >30 mL/min/1.73m²).
Consider GLP-1 Receptor Agonist (GLP-1 RA) or SGLT2 Inhibitor (SGLT2i) early, especially in patients with established ASCVD, heart failure, or chronic kidney disease.
Individualize medication selection based on comorbidities, glucose-lowering efficacy, hypoglycemia risk, impact on body weight, and cost.
Initiate ACE inhibitor or ARB for hypertension with albuminuria.
Statin therapy for dyslipidemia (moderate-intensity for adults 40-75 years without ASCVD, high-intensity for those with ASCVD).
Aspirin therapy (75-162 mg/day) for secondary prevention in patients with established ASCVD; consider for primary prevention in select high-risk patients.
Annual comprehensive dilated eye examination.
Annual foot examination (monofilament test, vibratory sensation, pulses).
Annual urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio (UACR) and estimated glomerular filtration rate (eGFR).
Referral to diabetes self-management education and support (DSMES) program.

medications.name: Metformin
medications.dosage: 500 mg once or twice daily, titrate up to 2000 mg daily (max 2550 mg daily) as tolerated.
medications.instructions: Start low and titrate slowly to minimize gastrointestinal side effects. Discontinue if eGFR <30 mL/min/1.73m². Hold prior to iodinated contrast procedures for patients with eGFR 30-60 mL/min/1.73m².

medications.name: Semaglutide (GLP-1 RA)
medications.dosage: 0.25 mg once weekly, titrate to 0.5 mg, then 1 mg, then 2 mg once weekly as tolerated.
medications.instructions: Administer on the same day each week, any time of day, with or without meals. May cause nausea, vomiting, or diarrhea. Contraindicated in patients with a personal or family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma (MTC) or Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia syndrome type 2 (MEN 2).

medications.name: Empagliflozin (SGLT2i)
medications.dosage: 10 mg once daily, may increase to 25 mg once daily.
medications.instructions: Take in the morning with or without food. Ensure adequate hydration. Monitor for genitourinary infections, euglycemic DKA, and volume depletion. Not recommended for eGFR <30 mL/min/1.73m² for glycemic control.

medications.name: Lisinopril (ACE Inhibitor)
medications.dosage: 10 mg once daily, titrate to target blood pressure.
medications.instructions: Monitor blood pressure, renal function, and serum potassium closely. May cause cough or angioedema. Contraindicated in pregnancy.

lifestyle: Adopt a balanced eating pattern (e.g., Mediterranean, DASH, plant-based) focusing on whole foods, lean protein, and healthy fats.
Limit intake of refined carbohydrates, added sugars, and saturated/trans fats.
Engage in at least 150 minutes per week of moderate-intensity aerobic physical activity (e.g., brisk walking, swimming).
Incorporate 2-3 sessions per week of resistance training (e.g., lifting weights, bodyweight exercises).
Achieve and maintain a healthy body weight; aim for a 5-10% weight loss if overweight or obese.
Prioritize 7-9 hours of quality sleep per night.
Manage stress through techniques such as mindfulness, meditation, or yoga.
Avoid smoking and limit alcohol consumption.

followUp.frequency: Every 3-6 months initially, then every 6-12 months once stable and at goal, or more frequently if glycemic targets are not met or complications arise.
metrics: Fasting Plasma Glucose (FPG) and Postprandial Glucose (PPG)
Lipid Panel (annually or more frequently if not at goal)
Estimated Glomerular Filtration Rate (eGFR) (annually or more frequently based on medication/CKD stage)

warnings: Metformin: Risk of lactic acidosis (rare but serious); contraindicated in severe renal impairment (eGFR <30 mL/min/1.73m²).
SGLT2 Inhibitors: Risk of genitourinary infections, volume depletion, euglycemic diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA), and rare Fournier's gangrene.
GLP-1 Receptor Agonists: Risk of pancreatitis, thyroid C-cell tumors (in rodents), and gastrointestinal side effects. Contraindicated in personal/family history of MTC or MEN 2.
Insulin/Sulfonylureas: High risk of hypoglycemia; educate patients on symptoms and management.
Blood Pressure Medications (ACEi/ARBs): Monitor for hyperkalemia and acute kidney injury, especially with concomitant use of SGLT2i or diuretics.
Polypharmacy: Review all medications regularly to avoid drug-drug interactions and simplify regimens.
Hypoglycemia Awareness: Patients with long-standing diabetes or frequent hypoglycemia may lose warning symptoms (hypoglycemia unawareness); educate on proactive monitoring.
Sick Day Management: Educate patients on managing diabetes during illness, including medication adjustments and monitoring for dehydration/ketosis.

name: Hyperlipidemia Management Protocol (ACC/AHA 2018 Guidelines)
description: This protocol outlines an evidence-based approach to the diagnosis, treatment, and ongoing management of hyperlipidemia in primary care, adhering to the ACC/AHA 2018 Cholesterol Guidelines. It focuses on reducing atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease (ASCVD) risk through lifestyle modifications and pharmacotherapy.

templateData.duration: Initial phase: 3-6 months for lifestyle and medication titration. Ongoing: Lifelong management.
goals: Achieve a ≥50% reduction in LDL-C for patients with clinical ASCVD or primary LDL-C ≥190 mg/dL.
Achieve a 30-49% reduction in LDL-C for patients with severe primary hypercholesterolemia (LDL-C ≥190 mg/dL) who cannot tolerate high-intensity statins.
Achieve a 30-49% reduction in LDL-C for patients aged 40-75 with diabetes and LDL-C 70-189 mg/dL.
Achieve a 30-49% reduction in LDL-C for patients aged 40-75 with LDL-C 70-189 mg/dL and a 10-year ASCVD risk of 7.5% to 19.9%.
Maintain non-HDL-C levels below target thresholds based on individual risk assessment.
Improve overall cardiovascular health and reduce the risk of future ASCVD events.

items: Perform 10-year ASCVD risk assessment using the Pooled Cohort Equations for patients aged 40-75 without ASCVD or diabetes.
Educate patient on the importance of hyperlipidemia management and its role in ASCVD prevention.
Discuss shared decision-making regarding statin therapy initiation, considering patient preferences and potential side effects.
Provide materials on heart-healthy diet and physical activity.
Initiate appropriate intensity statin therapy based on patient's ASCVD risk category and LDL-C levels.
Titrate statin dose as needed to achieve target LDL-C reduction, monitoring for side effects.
Consider adding non-statin therapies (e.g., ezetimibe, PCSK9 inhibitors) for very high-risk patients who do not achieve sufficient LDL-C reduction on maximally tolerated statin therapy.
Monitor lipid panel (LDL-C, HDL-C, Triglycerides, Non-HDL-C) 4-12 weeks after statin initiation or dose change, then annually or as clinically indicated.
Monitor liver function tests (ALT) and creatine kinase (CK) before statin initiation and as clinically indicated for symptoms.
Assess patient adherence to medication and lifestyle recommendations at each visit.
Address any barriers to adherence, including cost, side effects, and understanding of treatment plan.
Evaluate for secondary causes of hyperlipidemia (e.g., hypothyroidism, nephrotic syndrome, obstructive liver disease, certain medications).
Consider specific guidelines for patients with familial hypercholesterolemia.
Manage hypertriglyceridemia, especially severe cases (>500 mg/dL), to prevent pancreatitis.

medications.name: Atorvastatin (Lipitor)
medications.instructions: High-intensity (40-80 mg) for patients requiring ≥50% LDL-C reduction. Moderate-intensity (10-20 mg) for patients requiring 30-49% LDL-C reduction or unable to tolerate high-intensity.

medications.name: Rosuvastatin (Crestor)
medications.instructions: High-intensity (20-40 mg) for patients requiring ≥50% LDL-C reduction. Moderate-intensity (5-10 mg) for patients requiring 30-49% LDL-C reduction or unable to tolerate high-intensity.

medications.name: Simvastatin (Zocor)
medications.instructions: Moderate-intensity (20-40 mg). Note: 80 mg dose is not recommended due to increased risk of myopathy, especially with certain drug interactions.

medications.name: Ezetimibe (Zetia)
medications.instructions: Add-on therapy for patients who do not achieve sufficient LDL-C reduction on maximally tolerated statin therapy, especially in very high-risk individuals.

lifestyle: Adopt a heart-healthy diet (e.g., Mediterranean, DASH diet) emphasizing vegetables, fruits, whole grains, lean proteins, and healthy fats, while limiting saturated fats, trans fats, dietary cholesterol, and refined carbohydrates.
Engage in at least 150 minutes per week of moderate-intensity aerobic physical activity or 75 minutes per week of vigorous-intensity aerobic physical activity.
Maintain a healthy body weight (BMI 18.5-24.9 kg/m²) and reduce waist circumference.
Avoid tobacco products; for current smokers, provide counseling and resources for smoking cessation.
Limit alcohol consumption to no more than 1 drink per day for women and 2 drinks per day for men.
Manage stress through relaxation techniques, mindfulness, or other coping strategies.
Ensure adequate sleep (7-9 hours per night) for overall cardiovascular health.

followUp.frequency: Initially 4-12 weeks after statin initiation or dose change, then every 6-12 months for stable patients.
metrics: Liver function tests (ALT) if clinically indicated or at baseline
Creatine Kinase (CK) if muscle symptoms develop or at baseline if high risk
Assessment of medication adherence and side effects
Review of lifestyle modifications and progress

warnings: Statins are contraindicated in active liver disease or unexplained persistent elevations of serum transaminases.
Statins are contraindicated in pregnancy and breastfeeding due to potential fetal harm.
Risk of myopathy and rhabdomyolysis increases with higher statin doses and certain drug interactions (e.g., strong CYP3A4 inhibitors with simvastatin/lovastatin).
Monitor for new-onset diabetes, especially in patients with pre-existing risk factors, though the cardiovascular benefits of statins generally outweigh this small risk.
Patients experiencing muscle pain, tenderness, or weakness should have CK levels checked.
Concomitant use of statins with fibrates (especially gemfibrozil) or niacin can increase the risk of myopathy.
Patients with severe primary hypercholesterolemia (LDL-C ≥190 mg/dL) require high-intensity statin therapy regardless of ASCVD risk calculation.
Avoid grapefruit juice with simvastatin, lovastatin, and atorvastatin as it can increase statin levels and risk of side effects.

name: Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease (GERD) Management Protocol
description: A comprehensive, evidence-based protocol for the diagnosis and management of Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease (GERD) in primary care settings, adhering to the 2022 ACG Clinical Guidelines for the Diagnosis and Management of GERD.

templateData.duration: Initial treatment phase: 8 weeks. Ongoing management: As needed, based on symptom control and clinical assessment.
goals: Achieve complete or significant resolution of typical GERD symptoms (e.g., heartburn, regurgitation) within 8 weeks.
Prevent complications of GERD such as erosive esophagitis, stricture, or Barrett's esophagus.
Improve patient's quality of life related to GERD symptoms.
Minimize reliance on pharmacotherapy through lifestyle modifications.
Identify and address atypical GERD symptoms or alarm features requiring further investigation.

items: Clinical symptom assessment (heartburn, regurgitation, dysphagia).
Trial of empiric PPI therapy for typical GERD symptoms.
Consider upper endoscopy for alarm symptoms (dysphagia, odynophagia, GI bleeding, weight loss, iron deficiency anemia) or failure of empiric PPI therapy.
Consider ambulatory reflux monitoring (pH or impedance-pH) for refractory symptoms despite PPIs or atypical symptoms.
Initiate Proton Pump Inhibitor (PPI) therapy once daily, 30-60 minutes before the first meal.
If symptoms persist, consider increasing PPI to twice daily.
Consider H2 Receptor Antagonists (H2RAs) for on-demand symptom relief or as an adjunct to PPIs, particularly for nocturnal symptoms.
Evaluate need for step-down therapy after 8 weeks of successful PPI treatment.
Educate on the chronic nature of GERD and the importance of adherence to lifestyle and medication regimens.
Discuss potential side effects of medications and strategies for mitigation.
Provide guidance on dietary triggers and portion control.
Counsel on when to seek immediate medical attention (e.g., severe chest pain, choking, persistent dysphagia).
Referral to gastroenterologist for endoscopy, advanced diagnostics (e.g., manometry, impedance-pH monitoring), or consideration of surgical options (e.g., fundoplication) for refractory GERD.
Consider adding alginates or antacids for breakthrough symptoms.

medications.name: Omeprazole
medications.instructions: Take 30-60 minutes before the first meal for 4-8 weeks. May increase to twice daily if symptoms persist.

medications.name: Pantoprazole
medications.instructions: Take 30-60 minutes before the first meal for 4-8 weeks. May increase to twice daily if symptoms persist.

medications.name: Esomeprazole
medications.instructions: Take 30-60 minutes before the first meal for 4-8 weeks. May increase to twice daily if symptoms persist.

medications.name: Famotidine (H2RA)
medications.frequency: Once or twice daily, or as needed
medications.instructions: For on-demand symptom relief, particularly nocturnal symptoms, or as an adjunct to PPIs. Take before meals or at bedtime.

lifestyle: Elevate the head of the bed by 6-8 inches using blocks or a wedge pillow (not extra pillows).
Avoid lying down within 2-3 hours after eating.
Identify and avoid individual trigger foods (e.g., fatty foods, spicy foods, chocolate, caffeine, alcohol, peppermint, citrus).
Eat smaller, more frequent meals instead of large ones.
Achieve and maintain a healthy weight (if overweight or obese).
Quit smoking.
Avoid tight-fitting clothing around the abdomen.
Limit consumption of carbonated beverages.

followUp.frequency: Initial follow-up in 4-8 weeks after starting therapy. Subsequent follow-up every 6-12 months for chronic management or as needed for symptom changes.
metrics: Symptom frequency and severity (e.g., heartburn, regurgitation, dysphagia).
Adherence to medication and lifestyle modifications.
Presence of new or worsening alarm symptoms.

warnings: Alarm symptoms (dysphagia, odynophagia, GI bleeding, weight loss, iron deficiency anemia) warrant prompt endoscopy, not just empiric therapy.

Long-term PPI use has been associated with potential risks including C. difficile infection, pneumonia, bone fractures, and kidney disease; use the lowest effective dose for the shortest duration necessary.
PPIs should be taken 30-60 minutes before the first meal for optimal efficacy.
Do not abruptly discontinue PPIs after prolonged use due to potential rebound acid hypersecretion.
GERD symptoms can mimic cardiac chest pain; rule out cardiac causes in patients presenting with chest pain.
Patients with Barrett's esophagus require endoscopic surveillance.
Consider drug interactions with PPIs (e.g., clopidogrel, methotrexate).
Referral to a specialist is indicated for refractory symptoms, alarm features, or complicated GERD.

name: Osteoarthritis Management Protocol
description: A comprehensive, evidence-based protocol for the management of Osteoarthritis (OA) in primary care, aligning with the American College of Rheumatology (ACR) 2019 Guidelines for the Management of Osteoarthritis.

templateData.duration: Initial phase: 12 weeks. Ongoing management: Lifelong.
goals: Reduce pain by at least 30% on a Visual Analog Scale (VAS) within 12 weeks.
Improve physical function (e.g., walking distance, ability to perform daily activities) by 20% within 12 weeks.
Increase joint range of motion (ROM) by 10 degrees in affected joint within 6 months.
Minimize disease progression and prevent joint deformity through sustained management.
Improve patient's quality of life as measured by validated questionnaires (e.g., WOMAC, KOOS).

items: Patient education on OA, self-management strategies, and prognosis.
Weight management for overweight/obese patients (target 5-10% body weight reduction).
Structured exercise program (aerobic, strengthening, balance, flexibility) tailored to affected joint(s).
Physical therapy referral for individualized exercise prescription, manual therapy, and assistive device training.
Occupational therapy referral for joint protection techniques and activity modification.
Topical NSAIDs (e.g., diclofenac gel) for localized pain.
Oral NSAIDs (e.g., naproxen, ibuprofen) for moderate to severe pain, used cautiously.
Acetaminophen for mild to moderate pain (consider limited efficacy).
Intra-articular corticosteroid injections for acute flares or moderate-to-severe pain not responsive to oral/topical agents.
Assistive devices (e.g., cane, walker) for ambulation support.
Bracing/taping for joint stability and pain relief.
Heat/cold therapy for symptomatic relief.
Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT) for chronic pain management and mood disorders.
Referral to Orthopedic Surgery for patients with severe symptoms, functional limitation, and failure of conservative management (e.g., consideration for total joint arthroplasty).
Referral to Rheumatology for atypical presentations, inflammatory features, or diagnostic uncertainty.
Referral to Pain Management for complex chronic pain not adequately controlled.

medications.name: Topical Diclofenac Gel 1%
medications.instructions: Apply to affected joint area. Wash hands thoroughly after application. Avoid contact with eyes and mucous membranes.

medications.name: Naproxen
medications.instructions: Take with food or milk to minimize gastrointestinal upset. Use lowest effective dose for shortest duration. Consider concomitant proton pump inhibitor (PPI) if GI risk factors present.

medications.name: Acetaminophen
medications.instructions: Do not exceed maximum daily dose. Be aware of acetaminophen content in combination products. Use cautiously in patients with liver impairment.

medications.name: Triamcinolone Acetonide (Intra-articular)
medications.instructions: Administered by a qualified healthcare professional into the joint space. Aseptic technique is crucial. Monitor for post-injection flare or infection.

lifestyle: Engage in regular low-impact exercise (e.g., swimming, cycling, walking) for at least 150 minutes per week.
Maintain a healthy body weight through balanced diet and regular physical activity.
Incorporate strength training exercises for muscles surrounding affected joints.
Practice joint protection techniques (e.g., using larger joints for tasks, avoiding prolonged static positions).
Ensure adequate rest and sleep to aid in pain management and recovery.
Utilize assistive devices (e.g., cane, walker) as needed for support and stability.
Apply heat or cold packs to affected joints for temporary pain relief.
Avoid activities that exacerbate joint pain or cause excessive stress on affected joints.

followUp.frequency: Every 4-6 weeks initially, then every 3-6 months once stable.
metrics: Pain intensity (e.g., VAS, Numeric Rating Scale)
Physical function (e.g., WOMAC, KOOS, 6-minute walk test)
Joint range of motion and stability
Patient satisfaction and quality of life
Presence of new symptoms or disease progression

warnings: Avoid chronic use of oral NSAIDs in patients with high cardiovascular risk, renal insufficiency, or gastrointestinal bleeding history.
Acetaminophen should be used cautiously in patients with liver disease; do not exceed 3000 mg/day.
Intra-articular corticosteroid injections should be limited to 3-4 per joint per year due to potential cartilage damage.
Opioids are generally NOT recommended for routine OA management due to lack of efficacy evidence and significant risks.
Glucosamine and chondroitin are NOT recommended due to lack of consistent efficacy.
Duloxetine should be considered only for patients with chronic widespread pain or significant contraindications to NSAIDs.
Exercise programs should be initiated gradually and tailored to individual tolerance to avoid exacerbating pain.
Patients should be educated on signs of infection post-injection (e.g., increased pain, redness, swelling, fever) and to seek immediate medical attention.
